title: Apple chutney
subtitle: Bramleys, onion, vinegar, sugar, spice.
excerpt: Cooking apples chopped and simmered slowly with onions, sultanas, cider vinegar, brown sugar, and a warming spice mix until thick, glossy, and dark. Matures for six weeks in the cupboard before opening. Makes about five 340 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Apple Chutney'. The Anglo-Indian chutney tradition arrived in British kitchens via the Raj; the British version dropped most of the chilli heat and leaned into autumn orchard fruit.

recipe.freezeNotes: Sealed jars keep 12 months and improve with age. Open jars keep four weeks in the fridge.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; the long simmer scales without trouble.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mature for at least six weeks before opening; the vinegar mellows and the spices integrate.

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-based; nothing else carries the long cook without scorching.
Five 340 ml jars with vinegar-proof lids — not standard metal lids, which corrode.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plastic-lined preserving lid that won't corrode in contact with the acidic vapours of a chutney or pickle. Regular metal jam lids rust within weeks; vinegar-proof lids keep their seal for years.

content.text: A kilo and a half of Bramley apples, peeled and chopped, simmered with two big onions, sultanas, cider vinegar, light brown sugar, and a warming spice mix of mustard seeds, ground ginger, allspice, and a small dried chilli. Stirred occasionally for 90 minutes until the mixture is thick enough to part around a wooden spoon. Five jars from the pan, six weeks in the cupboard, and the chutney's at its best.

Apple chutney is the gateway preserve: no jelly bag, no pectin worry, no setting-point thermometer. Just a heavy pot, patience, and a careful eye on the bottom of the pan so nothing catches and burns. Vinegar-proof lids are essential; metal lids corrode within months and break the seal.

items.prepNote: peeled, cored, chopped into 1 cm pieces

content.text: Sit {{onion}} of chopped onions in a stockpot over a low hob with 2 tablespoons of cider vinegar. Cook for 10 minutes until soft and translucent.
Add the apple, fruit, and vinegar

Tip in {{apple-bramley}} of chopped apple, {{sultanas}} of sultanas, the rest of the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, and {{light-brown-sugar}} of light brown sugar. Stir to dissolve the sugar over a low hob.

Stir in {{ginger-ground}} of ground ginger, {{mustard-seeds-yellow}} of yellow mustard seeds, {{allspice}} of ground allspice, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes if using, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine sea salt.

Bring to a steady simmer. Cook uncovered for 75 to 90 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes for the first hour and every 5 minutes for the last 20 to stop the bottom catching. The chutney is ready when a wooden spoon drawn through the pan leaves a clean line that doesn't refill with liquid.
Ladle into hot sterilised jars. Tap each jar on the worktop to settle the chutney; lift any air pockets out with a skewer. Seal at once with vinegar-proof lids. Label with the date.

Store in a cool dark cupboard for at least six weeks before opening. The chutney is edible from week two; it's transformed by week six. The vinegar mellows; the spices integrate; the apple sweetness softens.

items.symptom: Chutney burned on the bottom of the pan
items.cause: Heat too high or stirring too infrequent at the end.
items.fix: Low simmer. Stir every 5 minutes for the last 20 minutes; use a flat-edged wooden spoon to scrape the base.

items.cause: Cooked too short; not enough water evaporated.
items.fix: Pour back into the pan and reduce another 20 minutes. The spoon-line test is the right end point.

items.symptom: Metallic lid taste after a few weeks
items.fix: Use vinegar-proof lids only. Repack any affected jars into clean jars with proper lids.

items.cause: Not enough headspace; jam not hot enough at jarring.
items.fix: Discard the affected jar. Jar at full boil; leave 5 mm headspace; seal immediately.

content.text: Apple chutney is the British ploughman's-lunch companion, eaten with mature cheddar and bread, served alongside cold cuts and pork pie, spooned over a Welsh-rarebit toast, or stirred into a curry sauce. The Anglo-Indian heritage shows in the spice mix; the British orchard adapts it to autumn.
